content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


August 7, 2001




Timothy W. Boyer, Chief Counsel
State Board of Equalization
Post Office Box 942879
Sacramento, CA 94279-0082

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-01-106

Dear Mr. Boyer:

        This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of members of the
Board of Equalization regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

        What is the operative date of Section 85702 to candidates for statewide elective
office?

CONCLUSION

        The operative date of Section 85702 to candidates for statewide elective office is
November 6, 2002. Other provisions, however, of the Act in effect on or before January
1, 2001, also apply.




_____________
1 Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-106
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

Government Code section 85702, which was added as part of Proposition 34, states:

"An elected state officer or candidate for elected state office may not
accept a contribution from a lobbyist, and a lobbyist may not make a
contribution to an elected state officer or candidate for elected state
office, if that lobbyist is registered to lobby the governmental
agency for which the candidate is seeking election or the
governmental agency of the elected state officer."

Section 83 of Proposition 34 provides that this act shall become operative on
January 1, 2001. However, Chapter of the Government Code shall apply to candidates
for statewide elective office beginning on and after November 6, 2002. Specifically,
Section 83 states:

"This act shall become operative on January 1, 2001. However,
Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 85100) of Title 9 of the
Government Code, except subdivision (a) of Section 85309 of the
Government Code, shall apply to candidates for statewide elective
office beginning on and after November 6, 2002."

Accordingly, the effective date of the prohibitions contained in Section 85702 is
January 1, 2001. As the law currently states, however, the effective date for candidates
for statewide elective office is November 6, 2002. The term "statewide elective office"
includes members of the Board of Equalization. (§ 82053.)

Your question warrants discussion of the effects of the interaction between the
two operative dates pertaining to Section 85702. As discussed above, on the one hand a
lobbyist is prohibited from making a contribution after January 1 of this year. On the
other hand, a candidate for statewide office² is not prohibited from accepting such a
contribution until November 6, 2002. Practically speaking, this may be a distinction
without a difference, insofar as candidates for statewide office will not receive
contributions from the law-abiding lobbyists who are registered to lobby those
candidates, thus rendering the effective date discrepancy essentially meaningless.

While the Act does not provide any specific consequences to candidates for
statewide elective office who accept contributions from lobbyists who, in so doing,
violate Section 85702, Section 83116.5 prohibits anyone with filing or reporting
obligations, such as a candidate for office, from aiding or abetting another in violation of
any provision of the Act. Section 83116.5 states, in part:

"Any person who violates any provision of this title, who purposely
or negligently causes any other person to violate any provision of

_____________
2 Legislators are not statewide elective officers. Therefore, this section applies to them as of
January 1, 2001.

File No. A-01-106
Page No. 3

this title, or who aids and abets any other person in the violation of
any provision of this title, shall be liable under the provisions of this
chapter. However, this section shall apply only to persons who have
filing or reporting obligations under this title, or who are
compensated for services involving the planning, organizing, or
directing any activity regulated or required by this title, ... ."

Thus, a candidate's liability in the situation you pose depends on the
conduct engaged in by the candidate or his or her staff. Moreover, we caution
that the scope of our advice pertains only to the provisions of the Political
Reform Act and does not include, and we render no opinion about, the
applicability of other areas of law which may be implicated in the scenario you
posit.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: C. Scott Tocher
Staff Counsel,
Legal Division

CST:jg

l, 2001, also apply.
